You might be thinking, what does corporate rate mean? Well, here's what corporate rating means. So corporate rating is when somebody buys huge amounts of shares or stock in a public company and then gains voting rights so that they can influence the board decisions and the direction of the company. And so what they do is they're looking at different businesses and they're like, okay, I want to raid this one. So they start buying the stock off the public markets and then once they get a certain amount of stock, they're able to vote and have a lot of say and actually make decisions for the whole company. And then they can tell the company what to do, like trim this up, cut the expenses here or drop this business and focus on this one. They can do things like that. And corporate raiders such as Carl Icahn spend their time looking for flabby businesses that are poorly managed and have too much expense or are not aggressive enough with growth. And so on the left here, there's an image of Carl Icahn on the front cover of Time. It says master of the universe. This dude is a financial genius and I've learned a ton from him.

## [42:28 - 45:30]

And this guy has corporate rated companies that are like the biggest companies you've ever heard of in the entire world. This dude's a multi, multi billionaire. And this dude is probably the most powerful corporate raider in the world. And so what he does is he looks for flabby businesses. And what I mean by flabby is businesses that are poorly run. They've got two, their expenses don't make sense. Their expenses are really high, but they're not contributing at all towards growth and the business is just screwing around. They're not focused. They don't even really know what they're doing. And so Carl Icahn doesn't like buying good companies. He likes buying bad companies that can be turned good. So what he does is he looks at companies that are bad, flabby, buys them. Then he bullies the board because he's got so many shares he can get them to do what he wants. And then the board listens to him and makes all the changes. And then the company starts performing better because it made the right decisions. And then once the company is performing better, the stock price goes up. And then once the stock price goes up, Carl Icahn sells his shares. And that's how he makes money. I think you probably can, but it's still it's not the best way to bulk or it's not the best way to cut.

## [48:32 - 51:35]

It's like trying to go fast and slow at the same time. I mean, it's probably not the best way to go fast, probably not the best way to go slow. The best way to go slow is to go slow. The best way to go fast is to go fast. So the best way to bulk is to bulk and the best way to cut is to cut. And so that's kind of how bodybuilding works. And so what happens is these bodybuilders, they bulk up and they eat a ton of food and they lift all these heavy weights and everything. And they get pretty big. Their muscles get massive. And but what happens is they kind of get a bit fat too while they're doing that. And then they don't really care about it because they're bulking. And then what they do is when they get to their bulky size, they then cut. And that's when they start trying to get rid of the fat. And so they cut down the amount of carbs and everything they're eating. And they do like higher reps and not as much intense, heavy lifting, just to try and like, you know, burn off the fat. And when they work in that way, when they bulk and then cut and then bulk and then cut and then bulk and cut, they get bigger than someone who's trying to bulk and cut at the same time. And this is very true with business. When you're trying to grow, you can't be trying to cut. So in your business, if you're trying to go through a massive growth spike, you can't be trying to cut expenses at the same time because that doesn't really work. In order to grow, you need to add expense. And in order to like reduce expense, sometimes you hurt growth a little bit. That's kind of how it works. So I like to work in really two month cycles. So I'll do like two months of growing and I'll let the expenses blow up a bit and get a bit messy. And then after that, I'll come in and I'll spend one or two days looking over the financials and I'll do some cutting and I'll trim up the fat. And then we'll go back into growing again, then I'll come back, trim up the fat. And it's good to do this once every few months. And you want to do it at the absolute least, like once every six months. Because over time, as you grow, you're going to pick up additional flabbiness and you want to trim it off. And then you want to bring on some more flabbiness again. You don't want to avoid flabbiness altogether because that's how you grow. But you don't want to leave it on. I'm pretty sure you get what I'm trying to say here. You need flabbiness to grow, but then you need to trim it off and then add it on again. You just need to go through those cutting, bulking cycles. You want to make sure that the more money you make, you increase your standard of living, but it doesn't get stupid.
